title: Collections Quiz 🏆
description: Prove your data skills! Master Lists, Sets, Maps, and Iteration.

questions.question: Which Dart collection is an ordered line of items?
questions.explanation: In Dart, an array is called a List.

questions.question: What number does an index count start at in Dart?
questions.explanation: Like most languages, Dart uses zero-based indexing. The first item is at index 0.

questions.question:
Fill in the blanks: To create a List of exact type int:
___<int> nums = [1, 2, 3];
questions.explanation: The 'List' keyword is capitalized.

questions.question: How do you add an item to the end of a List called 'names'?
questions.explanation: The .add() method appends an item to the end of a List or Set.

questions.question: A Set automatically sorts its items alphabetically or numerically.
questions.explanation: A Set simply enforces uniqueness (no duplicates). It does not guarantee sorting order.
The Set instantly discards the second '1' because it is a duplicate.

questions.question: If you have a Map and you don't know the exact index, how do you retrieve the value?
questions.explanation: Maps rely entirely on their Keys (left side) rather than numeric indexes.

questions.question: What does this code do? `Map<String, int> scores = {'Alex': 5};`
options: Creates a Map where the Key is an int and Value is a String.
Creates a Map where the Key is a String and Value is an int.
questions.explanation: The type declaration <String, int> explicitly forces Keys to be Strings, Values to be ints.

questions.question: If you ask a Map for a Key that DOES NOT exist, it will crash your application.
questions.explanation: No, it simply returns `null` safely.

questions.question: How do you create a completely empty Map?
questions.explanation: Maps (and Sets) use curly braces {}.

questions.question:
What is the name of the dynamic loop that automatically traverses a List item-by-item?
The for-__ loop.
questions.explanation: The 'for-in' loop format is: for (String item in list) {}

questions.question: When iterating with `.forEach()` over a List, what happens?
options: It stops at the first item.
It takes a function and executes it for every single element.
It deletes the elements while reading them.
questions.explanation: .forEach() takes a callback function and applies it serially to everything in the collection.

questions.question: You can use `.forEach()` on a Map to instantly access both the Key and the Value in the same loop.
questions.explanation: Yes, map.forEach((k, v) { ... }) exposes both the key and value seamlessly.

questions.question: If you need to rapidly check if an item 'exists' inside a massive collection of 10,000 items, which is faster?
questions.explanation: A Set is heavily optimized for fast `.contains()` lookups using hash codes. A List has to check items one by one.

questions.question: Which of these collections allow you to directly update an item by its position like `items[2] = 'New';`?
questions.explanation: Only Lists have numeric positioning / indexing. Sets do not have indexes, and Maps use Keys.

questions.question: To add a new Key-Value pair to a Map, you simply do:  myMap['score'] _ 100;
questions.explanation: You assign values to new Map keys using single equals.

questions.question: You can have duplicate Values within a Map (e.g., {'Alex': 100, 'Sam': 100}).
questions.explanation: Yes. Only the KEYS must be unique. The values can be whatever they want, including duplicates.

questions.question: Which specific property tells you how many items are currently inside a List or Set?
questions.explanation: In Dart, .length returns the integer size of the collection.

questions.question: What does a Map look like?
questions.explanation: Maps use {} braces and pair data using colons.

questions.question: If you have a Map named profiles, passing an entirely new item into the map using an EXISTING Key will _____ the old value.
questions.explanation: Because Keys must be unique, assigning new data to an existing Key overwrites the old data.

questions.question: Which represents a Set?
questions.explanation: Sets use {} but do NOT have key-value colons. Maps use {} WITH colons. Lists use [].

questions.question: A List can contain other Lists purely by creating a List of Lists (e.g. List<List<int>>).
questions.explanation: Collections can be freely nested. This creates 2D arrays or matrices.
